lessons.title: Building a Reliable AI Agent System
lessons.summary: Learn why a reliable AI agent needs normal software, safety rules, and human control around the AI model.
outcomes: Explain the difference between an AI feature, an assistant, an agent, a workflow, and a multi-agent system.
Separate AI recommendations from rules and actions that normal code must control.
Identify trust boundaries: places where data or control moves between parts with different levels of permission.
Choose a simple, fixed workflow when an AI agent would add risk without adding enough value.

theory.heading: The AI model is only one part of the system
paragraphs: A large language model, or LLM, receives text and produces a likely answer. Its answer is probabilistic. This means that the same input can produce different answers at different times. Think of it as a C# method that can return a different reasonable result even when its arguments are the same. This is useful for understanding language, but it is not safe enough for every business rule.

The model does not automatically remember reliable information, control permissions, recover after a crash, or enforce business rules. Normal application code must do these jobs. An agent system is the complete application around the model. It includes instructions, data for the current request, approved functions that the model can ask to use, saved information, safety rules, checks, logs, and a screen for the human operator.

Imagine that a customer sends this message to an online shop: "I paid for my order twice." The AI model reads the message and sends this suggestion back to the shop's application: "I recommend refunding the second payment." The suggestion is only information. The model cannot move money and does not have access to the payment account.

Our application receives the suggestion and handles it in a fixed order. The model's job ends after it sends the suggestion. The following steps tell the same story again and attach the engineering name to each part.

exampleSteps.name: Check that the suggestion is complete — Pydantic
exampleSteps.description: The application checks that the model supplied an action, an order number, an amount, and a reason. In this example, the action is "refund" and the reason is "the customer paid twice." Pydantic is ready-made Python code that performs this kind of data check. An incomplete or incorrectly formed suggestion stops here.

exampleSteps.name: Apply the safety rules — policy code
exampleSteps.description: The application checks the shop's saved payment records and its fixed rules. For example, the records must show two payments for the same order, and the refund cannot be larger than the extra payment. Policy code is normal application code that contains these safety rules. The AI model cannot change them.

exampleSteps.name: Ask a person — human approval
exampleSteps.description: A refund moves real money. The application shows the order, amount, and reason to a shop employee. Nothing happens until that person approves the refund.

exampleSteps.name: Send the approved refund — payment adapter
exampleSteps.description: After approval, a small part of the application asks the payment company to return the money. This part is called the payment adapter. An adapter translates our application's request into the form understood by another system.

exampleSteps.name: Save what happened — audit log
exampleSteps.description: The application saves the model's suggestion, the safety decision, the employee's approval, the refund request, and the result. This time-ordered record is called an audit log. It helps us understand exactly what happened later.

theory.takeaway: Use the model for recommendations. Use normal code for safety rules, permissions, and real actions.

theory.heading: Choose how much freedom the software needs
paragraphs: AI systems can have different amounts of freedom. An AI feature performs one small task, such as summarizing an error message. An assistant talks with a user. The application saves earlier messages and gives them to the model again when needed. An agent can choose an approved function, ask the system to perform an action, observe the result, and decide what to do next. A workflow follows steps that developers have already written in code. A multi-agent system gives different responsibilities to several agents.

More freedom is not automatically better. Every extra choice creates more possible situations and more ways to fail. It can also increase cost and create new security problems. This is similar to choosing between a simple C# service method and software that can run many configurable sequences of steps. You should add the more complex design only when the problem needs it.

A deterministic system gives the same result when it receives the same saved information and input. A state machine is code that lists the allowed conditions of a system and the allowed moves between them. An online order can move from "paid" to "shipped," but a cancelled order must not move to "shipped." Fixed code is usually the better choice for payments, approvals, permissions, and physical-machine changes. Use an AI model when understanding language or creating a flexible plan gives clear value.
theory.takeaway: Give the system only the amount of freedom that the problem requires.

theory.heading: What production-ready means
paragraphs: A production-ready agent has clear limits. We call this bounded behavior. The system limits which tools the agent may use, how much time or money it may spend, and how many steps it may take. It also has a safe way to stop. These limits prevent one incorrect model decision from becoming an unlimited sequence of actions.

The system must also be observable, testable, and recoverable. Observable means that engineers can follow one request from the request sent to the model through the proposed action, approval, real action, and final check. Testable means that automated tests can prove important behavior. Recoverable means that the system can continue safely, try an operation again, or return to an earlier safe condition after a crash or network failure.

People are part of reliability. An operator needs a clear approval screen that explains the proposed action and its risk. The operator also needs useful audit records and an emergency stop. Finally, the system must be secure and affordable to operate. A clever demo is not production-ready when people cannot understand, control, test, or recover it.
theory.takeaway: Prove reliability with tests, limits, logs, recovery steps, and human controls.

workedExample.title: Keep the model behind a fixed safety boundary
workedExample.explanation: Read the function from top to bottom. The order input contains the shop's saved order and payment information. The recommendation input contains the refund suggested by the AI model. RefundRecommendation is a Python class that lists the information a valid refund suggestion must contain. Its model_validate method is the Pydantic check. The policy object contains the fixed refund rules and returns a decision. Rejected and PendingApproval are result objects that clearly tell the rest of the application what happened. The payment service is the only part allowed to ask the payment company to return money. This is similar to keeping permission checks in a C# service instead of trusting values sent by a client.

practice.title: Describe the boundaries of the printer system
practice.scenario: The course project uses a simulated 3D printer. A 3D printer is a machine that makes a physical object by adding many thin layers of material. A simulator is a program that behaves like the printer, so no real machine is needed. Create a simple description of the important parts of this system. A trust boundary is a point where data or control moves between parts that have different permissions. The AI model may recommend an action, but it must not go around or skip the fixed safety rules.
tasks: Add the AI model. Mark its output as untrusted. This means that the system must check the output before using it because it can vary.
Add the fixed safety-rule code and the printer command service as separate boundaries. The command service is the only part that may change the printer.
Add the human operator who has permission to approve high-risk actions.
Write down one situation where fixed code is safer and an AI agent should not be used.

commandGuide.terminal: On Windows 10 or 11, open PowerShell. On Ubuntu, open Bash in the Terminal application. First activate the project's virtual environment. A virtual environment is an isolated folder that contains the Python tools and packages used by this project.
commandGuide.why: Run the automated tests to check that your changes did not break existing behavior.
commandGuide.program: pytest is a Python program that finds and runs automated tests. It is installed in the project's virtual environment.
parts: pytest starts the program and searches the project folder for Python tests.
-q asks pytest to show shorter output. The letter q means quiet.
commandGuide.effect: The command reads the project and runs its tests. It does not permanently change Windows or Ubuntu. It may create temporary files inside the project so that later test runs can start faster.
commandGuide.successEvidence: The final line reports that all tests passed, for example: 8 passed. The exact number may increase as the project grows.
commandGuide.failureRecovery: If PowerShell or Bash says that pytest was not found, the terminal cannot locate the pytest program. Confirm that the virtual environment is active. You should see its name, usually (.venv), at the start of the terminal prompt. If tests run but fail, read the first failure message and note the test file and line number.

practice.starterCode:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import StrEnum


class BoundaryKind(StrEnum):
    MODEL = "model"
    POLICY = "policy"
    EXECUTION = "execution"
    OPERATOR = "operator"


class BoundaryOwner(StrEnum):
    AI_APPLICATION = "ai_application"
    FIXED_POLICY_CODE = "fixed_policy_code"
    PRINTER_COMMAND_SERVICE = "printer_command_service"
    HUMAN = "human"


class AccessLevel(StrEnum):
    UNTRUSTED = "untrusted"
    TRUSTED_DECISION = "trusted_decision"
    PRIVILEGED_ACTION = "privileged_action"
    APPROVAL_AUTHORITY = "approval_authority"


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ArchitectureBoundary:
    kind: BoundaryKind
    owner: BoundaryOwner
    access_level: AccessLevel


def production_boundaries() -> list[ArchitectureBoundary]:
    # TODO: describe the model, policy, execution, and human boundaries.
    return []
codeGuide: from enum import StrEnum brings Python's string-enum type into this file. An enum defines a fixed set of named choices, like a C# enum. StrEnum is available in the Python version used by this course.

BoundaryKind.MODEL is a named enum member. The text value "model" is written once inside the enum instead of being repeated throughout the application. This prevents magic strings in the boundary objects.

class BoundaryKind(StrEnum) means that BoundaryKind is an enum whose stored values are text. The enum gives the code safe names, while the text values are useful when data is saved or returned as JSON.
from dataclasses import dataclass brings the dataclass helper into this file. A dataclass is a Python class made mainly to hold data. It is similar to a simple C# record.

@dataclass applies that helper to ArchitectureBoundary. The @ symbol starts a decorator. A decorator changes how the class works. frozen=True means that code cannot change an instance after creating it.
kind: BoundaryKind is a type hint. It says that kind must be one of the named values in BoundaryKind instead of any text value.
-> list[ArchitectureBoundary] is a return type hint. It says that the function returns a list whose items are ArchitectureBoundary objects.
TODO marks the part that you must complete. Replace the empty list with the four required boundaries.

checks.label: Include the policy and execution boundaries

hints: Do not give printer credentials to the model. Credentials are secret values, such as a password or access key, that allow software to control the printer. The model should not send commands directly to the printer.
Safety-rule code decides whether an action is allowed. Printer command code performs the allowed action. Keep these responsibilities separate even if both currently run inside the same application.
practice.solution:
def production_boundaries() -> list[ArchitectureBoundary]:
    return [
        ArchitectureBoundary(
            kind=BoundaryKind.MODEL,
            owner=BoundaryOwner.AI_APPLICATION,
            access_level=AccessLevel.UNTRUSTED,
        ),
        ArchitectureBoundary(
            kind=BoundaryKind.POLICY,
            owner=BoundaryOwner.FIXED_POLICY_CODE,
            access_level=AccessLevel.TRUSTED_DECISION,
        ),
        ArchitectureBoundary(
            kind=BoundaryKind.EXECUTION,
            owner=BoundaryOwner.PRINTER_COMMAND_SERVICE,
            access_level=AccessLevel.PRIVILEGED_ACTION,
        ),
        ArchitectureBoundary(
            kind=BoundaryKind.OPERATOR,
            owner=BoundaryOwner.HUMAN,
            access_level=AccessLevel.APPROVAL_AUTHORITY,
        ),
    ]

quiz.question: Where must the shop enforce permission for a refund?
options: Only in the instructions given to the AI model
In fixed safety-rule code immediately before requesting the real refund
Inside the company that provides the AI model
In the colors used by the approval screen
quiz.explanation: Model instructions can guide a recommendation, but the model can misunderstand or ignore them. Fixed safety-rule code must check permission at the execution boundary. The execution boundary is the last controlled point before the payment service requests the real refund.

completionEvidence: A diagram of the system that names each trust boundary and who controls it
A short Architecture Decision Record (ADR). This is a file that records a technical choice and the reasons for it. Compare a fixed workflow with an AI agent.
A list of actions that the AI model is never allowed to perform directly
An automated test that proves one important boundary or policy rule

lessons.title: Reliable Rules and State Changes
lessons.summary: Use normal Python code to define allowed states, safe changes, clear errors, and repeatable tests before adding AI.
outcomes: List the valid states of a system and the allowed moves between them.
Create typed requests, results, and named errors.
Prevent a repeated request from performing the same real action twice.
Build a software simulator for testing success and failure without real hardware.

theory.heading: Write the rules before adding AI
paragraphs: Think about an online order. A new order can become paid. A paid order can become shipped. A cancelled order must never become shipped. Write these rules in normal code before asking an AI model to help with orders.

A state is the current condition, such as NEW, PAID, SHIPPED, or CANCELLED. A transition is a move from one state to another. A state machine is code that lists the allowed states and transitions. An invariant is a rule that must always remain true. For example, a cancelled order is never shipped.

The printer simulator uses the same idea. It is a program that behaves like a printer without controlling real hardware. It becomes an executable specification: code that shows the exact printer rules and can be tested.

exampleSteps.name: List the conditions — states
exampleSteps.description: Write every condition that matters. For an order, examples are NEW, PAID, SHIPPED, and CANCELLED.

exampleSteps.name: List the allowed moves — transitions
exampleSteps.description: Write which state may follow another. For example, PAID may move to SHIPPED, but CANCELLED may not.

exampleSteps.name: Block every other move — transition guard
exampleSteps.description: A guard is a fixed check that rejects an invalid move. The AI may suggest a move, but the guard makes the final decision.

theory.takeaway: Let AI suggest an allowed choice; never let it create or bypass the rules.

theory.heading: Make inputs, results, and failures clear
paragraphs: A command is data that clearly describes a requested action. StartPrint can contain a job identifier and the material to use. A typed command states the required fields and the kind of value allowed in each field. This is similar to a C# request record.

A result should say exactly what happened: completed, rejected, waiting for approval, or failed. An explicit error is a named failure such as InvalidTransition. Named results and errors are easier to test than False or a vague message.
The command should describe the business request without depending on HTTP or one AI library. An adapter can translate the same command for the simulator or a future printer connection.
theory.takeaway: Define the business data and errors before choosing the API or AI framework.

theory.heading: Make retries safe and actions traceable
paragraphs: A network can lose a reply after an action succeeded. The caller may send the same request again. An idempotency key is a unique request identifier. When the application receives the same key twice, it returns the first result instead of performing the action twice.
Save the request key, decision, state before the action, state after the action, and result. This audit record lets an engineer reconstruct what happened.
Replay means rebuilding the history from saved records. It does not mean performing every real action again. Before continuing interrupted work, compare the saved state with the current real state.
theory.takeaway: Give every important action a unique identity and save enough information to explain it.

workedExample.title: Guard a printer transition
workedExample.explanation: The transition table is deterministic and can be tested without a model.

practice.title: Implement guarded transitions
practice.scenario: Add a small state machine that rejects invalid transitions and keeps the original state unchanged after failure.
tasks: Define printer states with an Enum.
Create an InvalidTransition exception.
Implement a transition function with an explicit allowlist.
Add tests for valid and invalid transitions.
practice.starterCode:
from enum import StrEnum


class PrinterState(StrEnum):
    IDLE = "idle"
    PRINTING = "printing"
    PAUSED = "paused"
    CANCELLED = "cancelled"


class InvalidTransition(ValueError):
    pass


def transition(current: PrinterState, target: PrinterState) -> PrinterState:
    # TODO: reject a target that is not allowed from the current state.
    return target
codeGuide: PrinterState is a StrEnum: a fixed set of named text values. It prevents state names from becoming repeated magic strings.
InvalidTransition inherits from ValueError. It gives an invalid state change a clear error type that tests can target.
current: PrinterState and -> PrinterState are type hints for the input and returned state.
Build the new state only after the allowlist check succeeds. A failed transition must leave existing state unchanged.
hints: Map each source state to a set of allowed target states.
Do not mutate an object until validation succeeds.

quiz.question: Why build a simulator before adding an LLM?
options: To establish executable domain behavior and safe failure scenarios
quiz.explanation: The simulator is an executable domain specification and a safe environment for tools, agents, and failure tests.

completionEvidence: Typed printer state and command models
Audit record that reconstructs a state change

lessons.title: AI Instructions and Checked Responses
lessons.summary: Give the AI a clear job, save conversation data in the application, and check every response before using it.
outcomes: Explain why the application, not the AI model, must save conversation history.
Write instructions that define responsibilities, limits, and output format.
Check that an AI response contains required fields and allowed values.
Choose a model using task ability, risk, speed, privacy, and cost.

theory.heading: The application must manage the conversation
paragraphs: Imagine asking an AI model a question today and another question tomorrow. The model does not automatically remember yesterday's conversation. Each request contains the information the model may use, and the model returns one response. This request and response is called a model call.

Stateless means that the model itself does not own lasting conversation information between calls. If earlier messages matter, the application must save them and include the relevant parts in the next request.
Save only what the product needs. This lets the application control privacy, deletion, and retention, which means how long data is kept.

exampleSteps.name: Save useful facts — application history
exampleSteps.description: For a support conversation, save the order number and confirmed problem instead of assuming the model will remember them.

exampleSteps.name: Choose what is needed now — context
exampleSteps.description: Context is the information included in the current model request. Add only the saved facts relevant to the new question.

exampleSteps.name: Send one request — model call
exampleSteps.description: The application sends instructions, selected facts, and the new question. The model returns a response but does not own the saved history.

theory.takeaway: The application owns conversation data and chooses what the model sees each time.

theory.heading: Instructions must describe responsibility and limits
paragraphs: Instructions tell the model what job to perform, what evidence to use, what it must not do, when to ask for help, and how to format its response. A persona is the defined role and behavior of the model. It should say more than “sound like an expert.”

Not every piece of text has equal authority. The application controls its own instructions. A user request may be mistaken or malicious. Text loaded from a document or returned by another system may also contain unsafe instructions, so treat it as data.
Give every instruction set a version, just as you version code. A prompt is the text sent to the model. Changing it can change behavior, so test the new version against earlier cases before release.
theory.takeaway: Write instructions as a testable job contract, not as a request for a writing style.

theory.heading: Check the model response before using it
paragraphs: A schema is a precise description of required fields and allowed values. Pydantic checks data against a schema. It can reject a recommendation with no evidence or an action that is not in the approved enum.

Validation proves that the response has the expected structure. It does not prove that the response is true or safe. Treat the validated recommendation as untrusted input, then apply fixed risk and approval rules.

An SDK is ready-made code that makes another product easier to use. An agent SDK can reduce repeated code for model calls, approved functions, sessions, approvals, and tracing. Keep your own typed contracts and safety rules around it so changing the SDK cannot silently change who owns state or permission.
theory.takeaway: Validate the response structure first, then make a separate safety decision.

workedExample.title: Validate a recommendation contract
workedExample.explanation: The schema constrains the response but does not grant permission to execute it.
workedExample.code:
class RecommendationAction(StrEnum):
    OBSERVE = "observe"
    PAUSE_PRINT = "pause_print"
    REQUEST_REVIEW = "request_review"


class Recommendation(BaseModel):
    summary: str = Field(min_length=1, max_length=500)
    action: RecommendationAction
    evidence: list[str] = Field(min_length=1)
    confidence: float = Field(ge=0, le=1)
    requires_approval: bool

practice.title: Design the first advisor response
practice.scenario: Create a Pydantic contract for a printer advisor that cannot invent arbitrary commands.
tasks: Limit recommended actions with Literal or an Enum.
Require at least one evidence item.
Bound the confidence value.
Write an invalid-output test.
practice.starterCode:
from enum import StrEnum

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


class RecommendationAction(StrEnum):
    OBSERVE = "observe"
    PAUSE_PRINT = "pause_print"
    REQUEST_REVIEW = "request_review"


class Recommendation(BaseModel):
    # TODO: add length limits to summary and evidence.
    summary: str
    action: RecommendationAction
    evidence: list[str]
    # TODO: accept confidence values only from 0 through 1.
    confidence: float
    requires_approval: bool
codeGuide: RecommendationAction is a StrEnum. The model can return only one named action from this closed set.
BaseModel is Pydantic's base class for checked data. Pydantic validates untrusted model output before application code uses it.
Field(min_length=1) rejects empty text or an empty evidence list. Field(ge=0, le=1) keeps confidence between 0 and 1.
A valid Recommendation has the correct structure. It still needs factual checks, risk policy, and possible approval.
hints: Use StrEnum for the closed recommendation-action set.
Field constraints can reject empty evidence and invalid confidence.
practice.solution:
class Recommendation(BaseModel):
    summary: str = Field(min_length=1, max_length=500)
    action: RecommendationAction
    evidence: list[str] = Field(min_length=1)
    confidence: float = Field(ge=0, le=1)
    requires_approval: bool

quiz.question: What does a valid structured model response prove?
options: The action is safe to execute
The response matches the expected data contract
quiz.explanation: Schema validity proves shape and constraints, not factual correctness or execution permission.

completionEvidence: Trace containing prompt, model, and schema versions

lessons.title: Building the Application Around the AI Model
lessons.summary: Build one small agent turn yourself so you can see where information, permissions, actions, and checks belong.
outcomes: Identify the application parts needed around an AI model.
Build a small loop without depending on one agent framework.
Keep AI decisions separate from code that owns powerful permissions.
Save progress, check real results, follow requests through logs, and recover after restart.

theory.heading: The model needs a reliable application around it
paragraphs: Think of the AI model as one worker inside a larger service. The worker can read information and suggest a next step. The surrounding application supplies information, offers approved functions, saves progress, checks permissions, verifies results, and shows important decisions to a person.

This surrounding application is called the agent harness. Two applications can use the same model and still have very different reliability because their harnesses give different permissions, save different information, and perform different checks.
A high-level SDK can build parts of the harness. Before using one, build one small turn yourself. This makes it clear where messages are stored, where permission is checked, and why a loop stops.

exampleSteps.name: Prepare the request — context builder
exampleSteps.description: The application selects the instructions and facts needed for this one decision.

exampleSteps.name: Suggest a next step — model
exampleSteps.description: The model returns a proposal. It does not directly perform the real action.

exampleSteps.name: Check and perform — policy and gateway
exampleSteps.description: Fixed policy code decides whether the proposal is allowed. A gateway is the controlled part that performs an approved action.

exampleSteps.name: Confirm the result — verifier
exampleSteps.description: A verifier is normal code that checks the real result instead of trusting a success sentence from the model.

theory.takeaway: The model proposes; the harness owns state, permission, execution, and proof.

theory.heading: Keep deciding separate from doing
paragraphs: The control plane is the part that understands the goal, prepares context, plans, and proposes actions. The execution plane is the part that owns secret access values and performs approved actions that change another system.
Keeping these parts separate limits damage. Unsafe text may influence a proposal, but it cannot directly become a command with powerful permissions.

Pass a typed action record between the two parts. It should contain the requester, action, arguments, risk, approval evidence, unique request key, and trace identifier used to follow the request through logs.
theory.takeaway: The part that reasons should not hold the credentials used for real actions.

theory.heading: Save progress and verify completion
paragraphs: Long work may wait for a person or continue after the application restarts. Durable state is information saved outside the running program so it survives a restart. Save the plan, completed actions, results, remaining budget, and pending approval.
Before resuming, compare the saved information with the external system. This comparison is called reconciliation. It prevents the application from continuing from an old or incorrect assumption.
Verification is a separate check that proves the expected result happened. Never mark work complete only because the model wrote a confident success message.
theory.takeaway: Completion means the expected result was observed and checked.

workedExample.title: A minimal bounded harness loop
workedExample.explanation: The loop proposes, validates, executes through a gateway, observes, and verifies.

practice.title: Build the loop before adding an SDK
practice.scenario: Implement one loop iteration using replaceable model, policy, gateway, and verifier protocols.
tasks: Define Protocol interfaces for each harness boundary.
Pass typed actions between components.
Enforce a step budget.
Return an explicit escalated result when the budget expires.
Implement the same typed turn once with the framework-neutral harness and once with the current OpenAI Agents SDK.
codeGuide: Protocol describes behavior that another class must provide. It is similar to a small C# interface and keeps the harness independent from one SDK.
The ellipsis (...) means the protocol declares a method signature but does not implement it.
Action.model_validate should convert the model's untrusted dictionary into a checked Action before policy sees it.
The loop must return distinct typed results for completion, escalation, and failure instead of a magic status string.
hints: Keep the model interface unaware of printer credentials.
A loop result should distinguish completion, escalation, and failure.

quiz.question: Why build a minimal loop before adopting a high-level agent SDK?
options: To understand and preserve the reliability and security seams hidden by abstractions
quiz.explanation: First-principles work exposes state, permissions, loops, and verification so SDK convenience does not dictate architecture.

completionEvidence: Trace from proposal through verified result
SDK equivalence report covering state, tool dispatch, tracing, verification, and framework coupling

lessons.title: Safe Tools, Permissions, and Approval
lessons.summary: Give the AI only the functions it needs, check every permission in code, and bind approval to one exact action.
outcomes: Describe every tool with typed inputs, results, permissions, and failures.
Separate tools that read, suggest, change data, need special permission, or are forbidden.
Check human approval immediately before the real action.
Handle timeouts, safe retries, incomplete failures, and untrusted returned data.

theory.heading: An AI tool is permission to do something
paragraphs: Imagine a support assistant with two buttons: “read order” and “refund order.” Reading changes nothing. Refunding moves money. Giving both buttons to the AI model would give it two very different levels of power.

A tool is an approved function that the model may ask the application to use. A capability is the power the tool provides. Each tool needs a clear input schema, owner, permission level, time limit, and named failures.
Start with no tools allowed. Add only the tools required for the task. This is deny by default. An allowlist is the small list of permitted tools. Keep forbidden actions completely outside the list.

exampleSteps.name: Find the requested function — tool registry
exampleSteps.description: A registry is a controlled list of tools. It rejects a tool name that was never approved.

exampleSteps.name: Check the requested power — execution policy
exampleSteps.description: Fixed rules decide whether the tool reads, writes, needs approval, or is forbidden.

exampleSteps.name: Perform an allowed request — gateway
exampleSteps.description: The gateway performs the checked action with narrowly scoped credentials. The model never receives those secrets.

theory.takeaway: Do not expose a capability unless the application can enforce its limits.

theory.heading: Approval must match one exact action
paragraphs: Suppose an employee approves a refund of €20 for order 123. That approval must not be reusable for €200 or another order. Store the exact command, requester, approver, risk, expiry time, and decision.

A fingerprint is a value calculated from the command and its arguments. If anything changes after approval, the fingerprint changes and the approval becomes invalid. Check it immediately before the real action.

Approval does not make unsafe access safe. People can become tired and approve misleading requests. Keep permissions small and enforce them in code. An SDK guardrail can reject unsuitable text, but it does not replace permission checks.
theory.takeaway: Bind approval to one unchangeable command and check it at the last moment.

theory.heading: Do not guess what happened after a timeout
paragraphs: A timeout means the application stopped waiting for a reply. It does not mean the external action failed. A refund may have completed even though the response was lost.
Before retrying an action that changes data, ask the external system for its current state. This is reconciliation. Use a unique request key so the system can detect a duplicate.
Treat returned data as untrusted. Check its structure, convert it into your own typed result, and label it as external data before giving it to the model.
theory.takeaway: Check the real state before repeating an action whose result is unknown.

workedExample.title: Authorize an immutable command
workedExample.explanation: The gateway recalculates policy and verifies approval before execution.

practice.title: Create the safe tool gateway
practice.scenario: Route printer tools through a registry that blocks forbidden tools and verifies approval fingerprints for high-risk commands.
tasks: Define a tool registry with risk metadata.
Reject missing and forbidden tools.
Require approval for high-risk commands.
Pass an idempotency key to the adapter.
Test the same denied action against an SDK guardrail and the deterministic execution policy.
codeGuide: __init__ receives the registry, policy, and approval service as dependencies. This is constructor injection, like passing interfaces into a C# constructor.
approval=None means the caller may omit approval for low-risk actions. Policy must decide whether that is acceptable.
Look up the tool and evaluate policy before using credentials or performing a side effect.
Pass the command's idempotency key to the adapter so a duplicate request cannot silently repeat the action.
hints: Fingerprint the tool name and normalized arguments.
Re-evaluate policy at execution time.

quiz.question: A write tool times out. What is the safest first response?
options: Reconcile the real target state before deciding whether to retry
quiz.explanation: A timeout leaves the outcome unknown; reconciliation prevents duplicate side effects.

completionEvidence: Typed tool registry and risk metadata
Timeout, duplicate request, and reconciliation scenarios

lessons.title: Safe AI Loops with Clear Limits
lessons.summary: Let an agent take several steps only when the application limits cost and time, checks progress, and knows when to stop.
outcomes: Compare acting after each observation, planning first, learning from failure, and exploring alternatives.
Create fixed stopping rules and progress checks.
Detect repeated actions and work that is no longer progressing.
Use a fixed workflow when flexible planning adds risk without useful value.

theory.heading: A loop needs visible steps and a reason to stop
paragraphs: Imagine looking for a lost package. First check the tracking page. Then use the result to decide whether to check the delivery address or contact the carrier. This is a loop: choose an action, observe the result, and decide what comes next.

ReAct is the name for alternating an action and an observation. Planning writes the steps before acting. Reflection uses a failed result to change the next attempt. Search explores several possible plans. Record short plans, actions, observations, and decision summaries. Do not store private hidden reasoning.

Begin with one structured response when it solves the problem. More branches and retries increase time, cost, and ways to fail. Use more complex patterns only when evaluations show a real improvement.

exampleSteps.name: Choose one safe check — action
exampleSteps.description: For the missing package, read the tracking status. Do not start with several unrelated actions.

exampleSteps.name: Save the returned fact — observation
exampleSteps.description: Record that the carrier marked the package delivered at 14:00.

exampleSteps.name: Check progress — verifier
exampleSteps.description: If the package is still missing, choose another approved check or ask a person for help.

theory.takeaway: Use the simplest visible reasoning pattern that solves the task.

theory.heading: Set limits before the loop starts
paragraphs: Every loop needs a maximum number of steps, maximum running time, model-usage limit, cost limit, and tool-use limit. Store these limits in normal application state so they survive a restart.
Stop when fixed verification proves the goal, when a person must decide, or when a limit is reached. Do not stop only because the model says it is finished.
Reaching a limit is an expected result called budget exhaustion. Return the facts collected so far, the unanswered questions, and the safest next action.
theory.takeaway: Every loop must have saved limits and fixed stopping conditions.

theory.heading: Detect when the loop is no longer making progress
paragraphs: Stagnation means the loop is running but not moving closer to the goal. Warning signs include the same action, the same result, lower confidence, or the same unanswered questions.
Save a fingerprint for each recent action and result. A fingerprint is a stable value used to recognize the same content again. Stop or use a pre-approved alternative when repetition is detected.
Uncertainty must not increase permission. A stalled agent may ask for another read-only check or human help, but it must not gain write access.
theory.takeaway: When progress stops, reduce activity and ask for help; never increase authority.

workedExample.title: Detect a repeated action
workedExample.explanation: A bounded history prevents the agent from calling the same ineffective diagnostic forever.
workedExample.code:
fingerprint = action.fingerprint()
if fingerprint in state.recent_action_fingerprints:
    raise StagnationDetected(fingerprint)
state.recent_action_fingerprints.append(fingerprint)
state.recent_action_fingerprints = state.recent_action_fingerprints[-5:]

practice.title: Bound a diagnosis loop
practice.scenario: Implement a printer diagnosis loop that stops on success, budget exhaustion, repeated action, or human escalation.
tasks: Define a LoopBudget.
Track action fingerprints.
Verify a goal with a deterministic function.
Return explicit terminal result types.
Compare one-pass, ReAct, bounded Tree-of-Thought, and bounded Reflexion on the same evaluation cases.
codeGuide: LoopBudget is frozen because limits must not change silently while the loop runs.
Decimal represents money without the binary rounding behavior of float. Use Decimal for monetary limits.
The loop should store fingerprints in a set because membership checks are fast and duplicate actions are not useful.
Return a typed terminal result such as Completed or Escalated instead of a boolean or magic status string.
hints: Use explicit result classes rather than a single boolean.
Store budgets and fingerprints in durable state for resumability.

quiz.question: What should happen when an agent exhausts its loop budget?
options: Return evidence and escalate with an explicit terminal result
quiz.explanation: Budget exhaustion is a bounded outcome that should preserve evidence and route safely to an operator.

completionEvidence: Loop budget and terminal result types
Trace showing plan revision and safe escalation
Reasoning-pattern comparison covering success, variance, latency, cost, repetition, and unsafe proposals

lessons.title: Choosing Information for the AI Model
lessons.summary: Choose the smallest trustworthy set of instructions and facts needed for each AI request.
outcomes: Separate fixed instructions, loaded information, saved memory, and the final model request.
Select relevant information, shorten it safely, save it, and isolate smaller tasks.
Recognize unsafe, irrelevant, confusing, conflicting, or overlooked information.
Measure input size, repeated-input reuse, response time, cost, and result quality.

theory.heading: Choose what the model needs for this decision
paragraphs: Imagine asking a colleague to solve one customer complaint. Giving the colleague every email from every customer would make the task harder, not easier. The AI model has the same problem.

Context is all information included in one model request. Context delivery loads possible information. Context management chooses what to include, where to place it, what to shorten, and what to leave out.
A context manifest is a list of every included item. Record its source, trust level, reason for inclusion, estimated size, and any change made to it.

exampleSteps.name: Collect possible information — context delivery
exampleSteps.description: Load the current complaint, order status, refund policy, and recent messages.

exampleSteps.name: Choose useful items — context management
exampleSteps.description: Include the current order and relevant policy. Exclude unrelated customers and old policies.

exampleSteps.name: Record the choice — context manifest
exampleSteps.description: Save which items were included and why, so a test can reproduce the model request.

theory.takeaway: Build context deliberately; more text is not automatically better.

theory.heading: Use four operations to control context
paragraphs: Select means keeping only relevant information. Compress means replacing long detail with a shorter faithful summary. The summary must preserve safety rules, evidence, decisions, and unanswered questions.

Write means saving plans or findings outside the model request so they can be loaded later. Isolate means giving one smaller task its own limited context so unrelated information does not distract it.
Shortening or separating information can lose important facts. Test summaries with questions about early, middle, and recent information.
theory.takeaway: For every context change, state which information must be preserved.

theory.heading: Placement and stability affect quality, speed, and cost
paragraphs: Important information can be overlooked in the middle of a very long request. Place stable instructions and critical safety rules clearly. Offer only the tools relevant to the current task.
A prompt cache lets a model service reuse unchanged beginning sections of repeated requests. Reordering everything on every call can prevent this reuse.
Measure response quality together with input size, response time, cost, and cache use. Reducing text is not an improvement if the model loses a critical rule.
theory.takeaway: Optimize context for correct results first, then speed and cost.

workedExample.title: Build a context manifest
workedExample.explanation: Every context item declares why it is present and how much authority it has.
workedExample.code:
manifest = ContextManifest(items=[
    ContextItem(kind="instruction", source="policy/v3", authority=100),
    ContextItem(kind="current_state", source="printer/status", authority=90),
    ContextItem(kind="retrieval", source="manual/chunk/42", authority=70),
    ContextItem(kind="memory", source="incident/18", authority=30),
])

practice.title: Engineer a long-task context
practice.scenario: Given noisy printer logs, a safety manual, a stale plan, and episodic memories, construct a manifest that preserves authority and stays within budget.
tasks: Select current state and authoritative evidence.
Compress long logs while preserving anomalies.
Write the investigation plan to durable storage.
Isolate one research subtask and define its handoff.
codeGuide: ContextBuilder owns the rule for turning possible sources into one model request. Keeping it in one component makes the decision testable.
token_budget is the maximum input size allowed for this build. The builder must not silently exceed it.
Return a typed ContextManifest that records included sources and transformations instead of returning an unexplained string.
Rank authority and permission before relevance so an attractive untrusted source cannot replace approved policy.
hints: Rank current device state above remembered incidents.
Keep summary invariants and unresolved questions explicit.

quiz.question: Which operation keeps detailed work outside the prompt while preserving it for later use?
quiz.explanation: Writing durable artifacts preserves work without consuming every later context window.

completionEvidence: Context manifest for each model call
Token, cache, latency, and quality comparison

lessons.title: Finding Trustworthy Knowledge
lessons.summary: Find approved source material, connect answers to exact evidence, and refuse to guess when evidence is weak.
outcomes: Prepare documents for search without losing source identity or important structure.
Combine searches by meaning, exact words, and approved document properties.
Require exact source references and enough evidence for every answer.
Test relevance, evidence support, current versions, and malicious documents.

theory.heading: Know where every fact came from
paragraphs: Imagine asking, “How many days do I have to return this product?” The current shop policy is stronger evidence than an old customer comment. A system must not treat both texts as equally trustworthy.
Provenance means information about where a fact came from. Store the source, version, owner, date, permission rules, and deletion rules for every document.

When preparing documents for search, keep important structure such as warnings, tables, product numbers, and ordered steps. A chunk is one smaller piece of a document used for search. Each chunk must still make sense and point back to its source.

exampleSteps.name: Find possible sources — retrieval
exampleSteps.description: Search the current policy and other allowed documents for passages related to returns.

exampleSteps.name: Check source identity — provenance
exampleSteps.description: Keep the policy version, date, owner, and access rules with every passage.

exampleSteps.name: Prefer the stronger source — authority
exampleSteps.description: Use the current official policy before an old customer comment, even if the comment uses more similar words.

theory.takeaway: A search result is useful only when its source and authority are known.

theory.heading: Search by meaning and by exact words
paragraphs: Semantic search looks for similar meaning. Keyword search looks for exact words, numbers, or error codes. Hybrid retrieval combines both. Metadata filters remove documents with the wrong version, language, customer, or permission.
Reranking means ordering the remaining results for this question. A complex question may be split into smaller searches and then recombined.

A vector database stores numeric representations used for meaning-based search. Chroma is one example. Keep it behind your own retrieval interface so storage-specific code does not enter safety or answer logic.
theory.takeaway: Check permission first, then combine exact-word and meaning-based search.

theory.heading: Answer from evidence or say that evidence is missing
paragraphs: Grounding means connecting an answer to supporting evidence. Add a citation that identifies the exact source passage for every important claim.
Abstention means deliberately refusing to answer when evidence is missing, old, conflicting, or unrelated. A clear refusal is safer than a confident guess.
Test search separately from answer writing. This reveals whether a failure came from selecting the wrong evidence or using good evidence incorrectly.
theory.takeaway: A clear unsupported-answer refusal is better than a fluent guess.

workedExample.title: Rank evidence by relevance and authority
workedExample.explanation: A simple score combines retrieval relevance with source authority and freshness.

practice.title: Build a grounded printer answer
practice.scenario: Retrieve safety guidance for a printer symptom and return citations only when evidence is sufficient.
tasks: Filter by printer model and source access.
Combine semantic and keyword candidates.
Rerank by relevance and authority.
Abstain below an evidence threshold.
Implement Chroma as one replaceable vector-retrieval adapter and verify it with contract tests.
codeGuide: retriever is an injected dependency. The function depends on your retrieval contract, not directly on Chroma or another database.
Apply access and printer-model filters before retrieved text can reach the model.
Return a typed GroundedAnswer with citations or a typed Abstention. Do not return an unsupported text answer.
Keep the evidence threshold in versioned configuration so evaluations can identify which value produced a result.
hints: Run authorization and tenancy filters before sending text to the model.
Return citation identifiers in the structured response.

quiz.question: Why evaluate retrieval separately from answer generation?
options: To identify whether failures come from evidence selection or synthesis
quiz.explanation: Separate metrics reveal whether the system selected poor evidence or misused good evidence.

completionEvidence: Relevance, groundedness, stale-source, and malicious-document evals

lessons.title: Saving and Forgetting Useful Memory
lessons.summary: Save useful information with clear purpose and lifetime, while ensuring current trusted facts always win.
outcomes: Separate current-task information, past events, lasting facts, and procedures.
Define rules for saving, reading, combining, expiring, and deleting memory.
Resolve conflicts using source authority, current facts, confidence, and time.
Test usefulness, unsafe influence, private-data exposure, and complete deletion.

theory.heading: Store different kinds of memory separately
paragraphs: Think about a support worker. The open case on the desk is different from a past case, a company fact, or a written procedure. Mixing all four together makes it hard to know which information is current.
Working memory holds the current task. Episodic memory stores past events. Semantic memory stores lasting facts. Procedural memory stores instructions for doing work.
Do not save every message forever. Decide why a memory is useful, who may read it, how long it should remain, and where it came from.

exampleSteps.name: Keep the open case — working memory
exampleSteps.description: Store the current order number and unresolved question only while the case is active.

exampleSteps.name: Keep a useful past event — episodic memory
exampleSteps.description: Store that a similar delivery problem was solved by correcting the address.

exampleSteps.name: Keep an approved procedure — procedural memory
exampleSteps.description: Store the current company steps for verifying an address, with their version and owner.

theory.takeaway: Choose the memory type, purpose, and lifetime before choosing storage.

theory.heading: Current trusted facts must beat old memories
paragraphs: A past order may contain an old delivery address while the customer's verified profile contains a new one. Similarity or age alone should not choose the winner.

Use fixed rules to rank sources. Current observed data and approved policy normally outrank remembered experience. Keep the losing record when it matters for audit, but state which source won and why.
theory.takeaway: Memory may inform a decision, but it must not silently replace current trusted data.

theory.heading: A safe memory system must forget
paragraphs: Retention is the rule for how long data is kept. Deleting old or low-value memories reduces cost, privacy risk, and the chance that old facts influence a new decision.
When a source is deleted, also delete summaries and search-index entries created from it. A negative test proves that deleted or expired data can no longer be found or affect an answer.
theory.takeaway: Deletion is complete only when the data cannot be found or used again.

workedExample.title: Resolve a memory conflict
workedExample.explanation: The resolver ranks current state and policy above episodic experience.

practice.title: Implement a memory policy
practice.scenario: Store printer incidents, retrieve relevant experience, and prevent it from overruling current safety policy.
tasks: Define typed memory records with provenance.
Implement authority-based conflict resolution.
Apply retention and tenant filters.
Add a deletion and non-recall test.
practice.starterCode:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime
from enum import StrEnum


class MemoryKind(StrEnum):
    WORKING = "working"
    EPISODIC = "episodic"
    SEMANTIC = "semantic"
    PROCEDURAL = "procedural"


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class MemoryRecord:
    kind: MemoryKind
    content: str
    source: str
    confidence: float
    observed_at: datetime


def resolve_memory(records, current_state, policy):
    # TODO: apply retention, access, authority, and conflict rules.
    raise NotImplementedError
codeGuide: MemoryKind is a StrEnum because memory type is a closed domain set, not arbitrary text.
MemoryRecord is frozen so saved evidence cannot be changed in place after creation.
observed_at records when the fact was observed. source records where it came from; neither value alone proves authority.
Filter expired and unauthorized records before ranking candidates, and return conflict details with the winner.
hints: Filter unauthorized and expired records before ranking.
Return conflict metadata, not only the winning value.

quiz.question: An episodic memory conflicts with current printer state. Which should win?
options: The episodic memory because it is personalized
Current observed state, subject to authoritative policy
quiz.explanation: Current state and authoritative policy outrank remembered experience.

completionEvidence: Typed memory schema and lifecycle policy

lessons.title: Reusable Agent Procedures
lessons.summary: Package one repeatable procedure with clear triggers, required inputs, steps, limits, checks, and versions.
outcomes: Explain how a reusable procedure differs from instructions, tools, fixed workflows, and memory.
Define when a procedure runs, what it needs, what it returns, and how it fails.
Load full instructions only after a small description matches the task.
Assign ownership, version, measure, retire, and roll back procedures.

theory.heading: A skill is a reusable procedure
paragraphs: Imagine that a support team has a checklist for reviewing a damaged-package claim. The checklist says when to use it, which photos and order details are required, which steps to follow, and when to stop and ask a manager.
A skill packages this repeatable procedure for an agent. It declares its trigger, required inputs, conditions that must already be true, steps, allowed tools, output, checks, and failure behavior.
A prompt is text sent to a model. A tool performs one capability. A workflow fixes a sequence in code. A skill combines instructions and supporting files for a focused class of tasks.

exampleSteps.name: Recognize the situation — trigger
exampleSteps.description: Activate the damaged-package skill only when a claim needs evidence review.

exampleSteps.name: Check required information — preconditions
exampleSteps.description: Require an order number and photos before starting the procedure.

exampleSteps.name: Return a checked result — output contract
exampleSteps.description: Produce a typed review result or a clear missing-information result, not an uncontrolled action.

theory.takeaway: A reliable skill states when it runs, when it must not run, and what result it returns.

theory.heading: Load the full procedure only when needed
paragraphs: Loading every procedure into every model request adds unrelated text and can confuse tool selection. Progressive loading first shows only a small name and description.
After the request matches the trigger and policy allows the skill, load its full instructions, examples, templates, and references. Record the selected version, reason, loaded files, and cost.
theory.takeaway: Show small descriptions first; load one full procedure only when it matches.

theory.heading: Version and test skills like code
paragraphs: Every skill needs an owner, version, change history, supported environment, tests, and a retirement plan. A stale skill can repeat an old procedure with confidence.
Measure how often the skill activates correctly, activates incorrectly, completes its task, and how much time and model usage it costs. Test failed cases before releasing a changed version.
theory.takeaway: Release a skill change only after tests, evaluation, and a rollback plan.

workedExample.title: Define a narrow incident-review skill
workedExample.explanation: The metadata can be discovered without loading the full procedure.
workedExample.code:
skill = SkillManifest(
    name="printer-incident-review",
    version="1.0.0",
    trigger="A completed or failed printer incident needs evidence review",
    required_inputs=["incident_id", "audit_log"],
    output_schema=IncidentReview,
    forbidden_actions=["execute_printer_command"],
)

practice.title: Package a printer-incident skill
practice.scenario: Create skill metadata and a loader that activates only for evidence-review requests.
tasks: Define a narrow trigger and explicit non-goals.
Declare required inputs and output schema.
Load full instructions only after activation.
Record version and activation evidence.
codeGuide: discover returns compact metadata only. It must not load every skill's full instructions into context.
load requires both a stable skill name and an exact version so behavior is reproducible.
The registry must check required inputs and policy before returning an approved bundle.
Use typed SkillMetadata and SkillBundle results so discovery data cannot be confused with loaded instructions.
hints: Discovery results should be much smaller than the loaded skill.
Reject activation when required inputs are missing.

quiz.question: Why use progressive loading for skills?
options: To expose compact metadata first and load full procedures only when relevant
To let every skill access every tool
quiz.explanation: Progressive loading controls context cost and reduces irrelevant procedure interference.

lessons.title: Connecting Agents and External Systems
lessons.summary: Connect agents to tools, data, other agents, audio, and images through checked and permission-controlled boundaries.
outcomes: Explain how MCP lets an application discover tools, data, and reusable prompts.
Verify identity, permission, allowed capabilities, data structure, and versions.
Send typed tasks between agents without passing all credentials or permissions.
Treat information extracted from audio and images as uncertain evidence.

theory.heading: A standard connection does not make a server trustworthy
paragraphs: A USB plug has a standard shape, but you still decide whether to trust the device you connect. A software protocol works in the same way: a shared format helps systems communicate but does not grant trust.

Model Context Protocol, or MCP, is a standard way for an AI application to discover and use tools, data resources, and reusable prompts from a server. The client must still verify identity, permission, input data, time limits, and returned data.

For a local server, stdio means reading and writing through the started program's standard input and output. For a remote server, Streamable HTTP sends messages over the network. Remote connections also need client authentication, allowed web origins, and recovery after a lost session.

exampleSteps.name: Ask what is available — discovery
exampleSteps.description: The client requests the server's capability list but keeps only locally allowed entries.

exampleSteps.name: Check identity and permission — authentication and authorization
exampleSteps.description: Authentication proves who the caller is. Authorization checks what that caller may do.

exampleSteps.name: Check both directions — schema validation
exampleSteps.description: Validate arguments before the call and validate returned data before the agent uses it.

theory.takeaway: A protocol provides communication; your application still provides security.

theory.heading: Agents should exchange small typed tasks
paragraphs: Agent-to-agent, or A2A, communication lets one agent send a task and receive a result from another. The handoff should contain the exact task, allowed capabilities, budget, input references, and expected result schema.
The receiving agent must not automatically receive the sender's full conversation, credentials, or permissions. Version the task and result schemas so incompatible changes fail clearly.
theory.takeaway: Delegate one typed task, not all of the sender's context and authority.

theory.heading: Audio and images are observations, not permissions
paragraphs: Multimodal means using more than text, such as audio or images. A model can mishear speech or misunderstand a picture, so extracted facts are probabilistic rather than guaranteed.

Keep a reference to the original file, the model version, confidence, and extracted facts. Apply privacy and deletion rules to sensitive media. An image may support a recommendation, but it never grants permission for a high-risk action.
theory.takeaway: New input types can add evidence, but they do not add authority.

workedExample.title: Expose a safe MCP capability
workedExample.explanation: Only approved read and bounded write capabilities enter the server allowlist.

practice.title: Design the printer MCP boundary
practice.scenario: Create an MCP adapter that discovers only allowlisted printer capabilities and treats server output as untrusted.
tasks: Define an explicit capability allowlist.
Authorize discovery and invocation.
Validate tool arguments and results.
Handle unavailable and malformed servers.
Expose the same read-only capability over stdio and Streamable HTTP, then document how a legacy HTTP-plus-SSE client would migrate.
codeGuide: The adapter hides the external MCP server behind an application-owned interface, similar to an anti-corruption layer in .NET.
identity tells authorization code who is requesting access. Authentication must establish it before these methods run.
discover filters the server list through the local allowlist before the model sees any capability.
invoke validates input, checks permission, calls the dependency, and validates output in that order.
hints: Filter discovery results before presenting them to the model.
Normalize errors into typed dependency failures.

quiz.question: What authority should a receiving A2A agent automatically inherit?
options: Only authority explicitly granted for the typed task
quiz.explanation: Handoffs should carry scoped task authority, not ambient credentials or permissions.

completionEvidence: MCP discovery and invocation contract tests
Transport comparison with Origin, authentication, session-loss, and legacy-migration tests

lessons.title: Using More Than One Agent
lessons.summary: Split work between agents only when it provides measured value and does not multiply powerful permissions.
outcomes: Compare work done in order, by a coordinator and workers, in parallel, or through free collaboration.
Send small typed tasks with limited information and permissions.
Keep approval and powerful execution in one controlled place.
Measure added time, cost, duplicate work, and new failure paths.

theory.heading: Add another agent only when separate work helps
paragraphs: Imagine preparing a small event. One person can do all tasks in order. For a larger event, one coordinator may give venue research and food research to separate workers. More people help only when the work can be separated clearly.

A sequential flow passes a result through roles in order. A coordinator-worker pattern gives tasks to workers and combines their results. Fan-out sends independent tasks at the same time. Free collaboration allows more communication but can create duplicate work, waiting cycles, and unclear ownership.
Every additional agent adds model calls, context, identity, tracing, cost, and failure paths. Compare it with one agent and a fixed workflow before keeping it.

exampleSteps.name: Split independent work — decomposition
exampleSteps.description: Give venue research and food research separate typed tasks only because they can be completed independently.

exampleSteps.name: Limit each worker — context isolation
exampleSteps.description: Each worker receives only its task, allowed tools, budget, and expected output.

exampleSteps.name: Combine checked results — coordinator
exampleSteps.description: The coordinator validates both results before using them in the event plan.

theory.takeaway: Use multiple agents only when measurements show an advantage over a simpler design.

theory.heading: A handoff must define one bounded responsibility
paragraphs: A handoff is the data sent when one agent gives work to another. Include the task, allowed capabilities, input references, budget, deadline, and expected output schema.

Do not copy the whole parent conversation. Context isolation means giving the worker only the information needed for its responsibility. Validate the returned artifact, which is the worker's saved result, before using it.
theory.takeaway: Agents exchange small typed tasks and checked results, not entire conversations.

theory.heading: Keep powerful actions in one controlled place
paragraphs: Several agents may research or review, but they should not each receive credentials for real actions. One execution gateway should apply identity, policy, approval, and duplicate-request protection.
Carry one trace identifier through every handoff. This lets an engineer reconstruct the complete task across all participating agents.
theory.takeaway: More planners must not create more privileged execution paths.

workedExample.title: Create a typed diagnosis handoff
workedExample.explanation: The worker receives a bounded task and returns evidence, not a side effect.
workedExample.code:
class DiagnosisTool(StrEnum):
    READ_STATUS = "read_status"
    SEARCH_MANUAL = "search_manual"


class DiagnosisTask(BaseModel):
    incident_id: str
    evidence_refs: list[str]
    allowed_tools: set[DiagnosisTool]
    max_steps: int = Field(gt=0, le=5)


class DiagnosisArtifact(BaseModel):
    hypothesis: str
    evidence_refs: list[str]
    confidence: float = Field(ge=0, le=1)

practice.title: Orchestrate a bounded incident team
practice.scenario: Create monitor, diagnostician, and safety-review roles while preserving one execution gateway.
tasks: Define typed task and artifact contracts.
Give each role the minimum context and tools.
Propagate correlation and budget identifiers.
Compare with a single-agent implementation.
codeGuide: IncidentCoordinator controls the sequence but does not give workers execution credentials.
Each arrow in the TODO represents a typed handoff: a task goes out and a validated artifact comes back.
Carry the incident correlation identifier and remaining budget through every handoff.
The final result is a proposal for the central execution gateway, not a direct printer command.
hints: The safety reviewer returns a decision artifact, not a printer command.
Record agent identity on every trace span.

quiz.question: What is the strongest reason to add another agent?
options: Measured improvement from specialization, isolation, decomposition, or parallelism
quiz.explanation: Multi-agent complexity is justified only by measured value over simpler designs.

completionEvidence: Single-agent versus multi-agent quality, cost, and latency report

lessons.title: Testing AI Agent Reliability
lessons.summary: Use saved test cases, repeated runs, separate safety checks, and release rules to measure agent behavior.
outcomes: Create versioned test cases, scoring rules, measurements, and identifiable test runs.
Measure final results, action paths, consistency, safety, cost, speed, and usability.
Repeat tests and distinguish occasional success from success on every required run.
Compare model-based scoring with people and detect behavior changes between releases.

theory.heading: Measure success and the way it was achieved
paragraphs: Imagine testing a support assistant. It may produce the correct refund answer but use a forbidden data source. The final answer succeeded, but the path was unsafe.

An outcome metric measures the final result. A trajectory metric measures the path, such as selected tools, arguments, order, and recovery. Reliability measures whether repeated runs behave consistently. Also measure safety, cost, response time, and whether a person can use the result.
Do not hide all results inside one average. A serious safety failure must block release even when average task success increases.

exampleSteps.name: Run a defined case — evaluation scenario
exampleSteps.description: Give the system a saved input with a known expected result and safety rules.

exampleSteps.name: Check result and path — graders
exampleSteps.description: A grader is code or a reviewed model that scores the answer and the actions used to produce it.

exampleSteps.name: Apply release rules — evaluation gate
exampleSteps.description: Block release when a critical safety check fails, even if other scores are high.

theory.takeaway: A good answer reached through an unsafe path is still a failed evaluation.

theory.heading: Run probabilistic tests more than once
paragraphs: Because model responses can vary, one passing run is weak evidence. Repeat the same scenario and keep every result.
Pass-at-k asks whether at least one of k attempts succeeds. Pass-to-the-k asks whether all k required attempts succeed. Use the second when the system must be dependable every time.
Count a safe refusal separately from a confident wrong answer. Abstention means the system clearly says it lacks enough evidence.
theory.takeaway: Measure consistency and distinguish safe refusal from confident error.

theory.heading: Make every evaluation reproducible
paragraphs: Use fixed code to grade schemas, states, tool calls, citations, and policy when possible. A model judge can grade qualities that fixed code cannot, but compare it with human labels and measure disagreement.

Save a fingerprint of the dataset, model, instructions, harness, tools, skills, policies, and results. A fingerprint identifies the exact evaluation configuration. Compare versions to find behavior drift, which means behavior changed over time.

A trace follows one run through model calls, retrieval, tools, approvals, and verification. Traces help explain failures but do not replace saved evaluation cases. Remove secrets and private data before sending traces to an external system.
theory.takeaway: An evaluation is useful only when its inputs, configuration, and results can be reproduced.

workedExample.title: Score safety separately from task success
workedExample.explanation: A scenario can complete its task and still fail the release gate.
workedExample.code:
result = EvalResult(
    task_success=1.0,
    tool_choice=1.0,
    approval_correctness=0.0,
    safety_policy=0.0,
)
release_allowed = result.safety_policy == 1.0 and result.approval_correctness == 1.0

practice.title: Build a reproducible eval run
practice.scenario: Evaluate a printer advisor across repeated trials, including a dependency failure and a confidently wrong recommendation.
tasks: Define versioned scenario items and deterministic graders.
Run each probabilistic scenario multiple times.
Track abstention separately from confident error.
Fingerprint configuration and apply a safety release gate.
Correlate evaluation failures with provider-native and OpenTelemetry-compatible traces while redacting sensitive data.
codeGuide: dataset is a versioned collection of evaluation cases. system is the exact agent configuration being tested.
Keep each trial result before calculating totals so failures and variation remain visible.
A run fingerprint identifies the dataset, model, instructions, tools, policies, and other configuration.
The release gate must check safety failures directly instead of allowing a high average score to hide them.
hints: Keep raw per-trial results before aggregation.
A safety gate should not be diluted by unrelated successes.

quiz.question: Which metric asks whether every one of several required runs succeeds?
quiz.explanation: Pass-to-the-k captures consistent success across all required runs.

completionEvidence: Safety release gate and drift comparison

lessons.title: Safely Improving the Agent Application
lessons.summary: Use repeated failure evidence to propose one limited change without letting the agent approve or release its own work.
outcomes: Find repeated weaknesses in saved runs, incidents, tests, and safe refusals.
Define which application parts may change and which must never change automatically.
Test a proposed change independently in a separated environment.
Require human review, gradual release, monitoring, rollback, and separate responsibilities.

theory.heading: Use repeated failures to propose one small improvement
paragraphs: Imagine that a support assistant repeatedly forgets the return deadline when long messages are shortened. The evidence suggests one narrow problem: the summarizer is dropping dates.

Weakness mining means grouping related failures and forming a specific explanation that can be tested. The system may propose a candidate change to the summarizer. It may not rewrite any part of itself without limits.
An editable surface is a part that the improvement process may change. Safety rules, credential policy, release gates, and the improvement process itself must remain outside that permission.

exampleSteps.name: Group repeated evidence — weakness report
exampleSteps.description: Link the failing cases that all lost an important date during summarization.

exampleSteps.name: State one testable idea — hypothesis
exampleSteps.description: Propose that preserving dates explicitly will fix the failure.

exampleSteps.name: Create a limited change — candidate
exampleSteps.description: Change only the approved summarization rule and send the candidate for independent testing.

theory.takeaway: Self-improvement means proposing a limited reviewable change, not changing production freely.

theory.heading: The proposer must not approve its own change
paragraphs: Build the candidate in an isolated branch or sandbox, which is a limited environment separated from production. Run tests for the specific weakness and all existing regression tests.
Separate four responsibilities: proposing, evaluating, approving, and deploying. Save the link from original failures to the code change, test results, human decision, and release.
theory.takeaway: No actor should create, judge, approve, and deploy the same change.

theory.heading: Test that the change improves real behavior
paragraphs: Overfitting means improving only the known test cases without improving the real task. Reward hacking means finding a way to score well without achieving the intended result.

Use unseen test cases, difficult cases, human review, and behavior-change measurements. Release first to a small canary group, compare it with the current version, and keep a tested rollback and emergency stop.
Run improvement cycles on a schedule or by explicit request. Do not rewrite production after every interaction.
theory.takeaway: The process that changes an agent needs stricter controls than the agent.

workedExample.title: Create a candidate-change record
workedExample.explanation: The record connects evidence to a narrow editable surface and forbids direct deployment.
workedExample.code:
class HarnessSurface(StrEnum):
    CONTEXT_COMPACTION_POLICY = "context.compaction_policy"
    EXECUTION_POLICY = "execution_policy"
    RELEASE_GATE = "release_gate"


candidate = HarnessChangeProposal(
    weakness_ids=["eval-42", "incident-17"],
    editable_surface=HarnessSurface.CONTEXT_COMPACTION_POLICY,
    immutable_surfaces={
        HarnessSurface.EXECUTION_POLICY,
        HarnessSurface.RELEASE_GATE,
    },
    proposed_patch_ref="candidate/compaction-v2",
    may_self_deploy=False,
)

practice.title: Implement the controlled improvement pipeline
practice.scenario: A recurring compaction failure drops safety constraints. Mine the weakness, test a candidate, and require independent approval before a simulated release.
tasks: Create a weakness report from multiple evidence records.
Restrict the candidate to one editable surface.
Run targeted and full regression evaluations.
Require approval and produce rollback evidence.
codeGuide: propose may create a candidate only for an allowlisted editable surface. It must reject changes to safety or release controls.
release receives independent evaluation and human approval as separate inputs; the proposer cannot manufacture either one.
Verify that immutable surfaces are unchanged before starting a canary release.
Return a typed release result that includes the rollback target and evidence references.
hints: Reject candidates that touch immutable surfaces.
Keep the baseline available until the canary passes.

quiz.question: Which authority should the proposing agent have over its candidate change?
options: Propose within allowed surfaces, with independent evaluation and approval
quiz.explanation: The agent may propose a constrained change but must not control evaluation, approval, or deployment.

completionEvidence: Weakness report linked to incidents and evals
Candidate patch limited to an editable surface
Approval, canary, kill-switch, and rollback proof

lessons.title: Security and Damage Limits
lessons.summary: Enforce limits on files, programs, networks, secrets, devices, resources, and access between customers.
outcomes: Explain why written AI instructions cannot replace technical access controls.
Compare several isolation technologies and the threats each one can stop.
Control file, process, network, secret, device, and customer access.
Limit maximum damage, clean temporary work, preserve security evidence, and respond to incidents.

theory.heading: A written rule cannot block technical access
