paragraphs: Telling a child “do not open this drawer” is not the same as locking the drawer. In the same way, a prompt that says “do not read secrets” does not remove file access.

Isolation means technical controls that limit files, programs, networks, credentials, and devices. Least privilege means granting only the smallest access needed for the task. Deny by default means access remains blocked until it is explicitly allowed.
Human approval confirms intent but cannot contain compromised code. Keep enforceable limits even when a person approves an action.

exampleSteps.name: Grant only required files — filesystem boundary
exampleSteps.description: Give the task read access to the project and write access only to a temporary folder.

exampleSteps.name: Block unneeded connections — network boundary
exampleSteps.description: Deny public network access unless one named destination is required.

exampleSteps.name: Limit resources — process boundary
exampleSteps.description: Set maximum running time, memory, and number of child processes.

theory.takeaway: Security comes from enforced access limits, not from polite instructions.

theory.heading: Different isolation layers stop different threats
paragraphs: A language isolate limits code inside one runtime. WebAssembly is a portable instruction format with a limited host interface. An operating-system sandbox restricts what a process may ask the operating system to do.

A Docker container packages a process and its dependencies but shares the host kernel. gVisor adds another boundary between the process and host kernel. A microVM runs a small virtual machine and gives stronger separation at higher startup and operating cost.
Choose layers from the actual threat, required software, speed, number of users, and data sensitivity. One layer does not solve every security problem.
theory.takeaway: Choose isolation from the threat and required access, not product popularity.

theory.heading: Decide where damage must stop
paragraphs: Blast radius means the maximum damage one failure can cause. Limit mounted files, network destinations, devices, CPU, memory, child processes, and running time.

Give one tool a short-lived credential for one request instead of placing a broad secret in the whole environment. Delete temporary workspaces after use and keep only approved results and security evidence.
A threat model is a written list of possible attackers, failures, targets, and defenses. Include malicious instructions, unsafe dependencies, stolen data, and access between customers.
theory.takeaway: Assume a failure can happen and enforce where its damage stops.

workedExample.title: Declare a sandbox profile
workedExample.explanation: The task receives only the resources required for a read-only repository check.
workedExample.code:
profile = SandboxProfile(
    filesystem=ReadOnlyMounts(["/workspace/repo"]),
    writable_paths=["/workspace/tmp"],
    network_allowlist=[],
    credentials=[],
    max_processes=8,
    memory_mb=512,
    destroy_after_seconds=900,
)

practice.title: Design and test a sandbox profile
practice.scenario: A lesson checker must read repository code and write temporary test output, but it must not access user files, secrets, or the public network.
tasks: Define read-only and writable filesystem scopes.
Deny network egress by default.
Avoid ambient credentials.
Set process, memory, time, and cleanup limits.
codeGuide: Return one typed SandboxProfile so tests can inspect every granted capability.
Use explicit read-only and writable paths. Do not use broad home-directory or filesystem-root access.
An empty network allowlist and empty credential list implement deny-by-default behavior.
Resource and lifetime limits must be values enforced by the sandbox, not comments in a prompt.
hints: Make the repository read-only and use a separate temporary write mount.
A timeout should trigger process termination and workspace cleanup.

quiz.question: What important limit does Docker have as an isolation boundary?
options: It removes the need for credential policy
quiz.explanation: Docker isolates processes but shares the host kernel, which matters for threat modeling.

completionEvidence: Filesystem, egress, secret, quota, and cleanup tests

lessons.title: Running Agent Systems in Production
lessons.summary: Save long-running work, follow each request, recover from dependency failures, release safely, and control cost.
outcomes: Choose whether work completes now, continues later, waits in a queue, or reacts to an event.
Save progress, prevent duplicate actions, resume safely, and compare saved state with reality.
Follow model calls, information loading, tools, approvals, handoffs, and result checks.
Define reliability targets, failure handling, release controls, and operating budgets.

theory.heading: Save long work before waiting
paragraphs: Imagine an online order that waits several hours for payment confirmation. The web request cannot stay open for hours. Save the order state, then let another worker continue when confirmation arrives.

Agent tasks can also outlive one web request. A checkpoint is saved progress from which work can safely continue. A queue stores work until a worker is ready. After interruption, reconcile saved state with the external system before continuing.
Use an idempotency key to prevent duplicate changes. A dead-letter queue stores work that repeatedly fails so a person can inspect it instead of losing it.

exampleSteps.name: Save before waiting — checkpoint
exampleSteps.description: Store the task state and exact pending action before asking a person for approval.

exampleSteps.name: Resume later — queue and worker
exampleSteps.description: A worker loads the saved task after approval or restart instead of relying on old memory.

exampleSteps.name: Compare with reality — reconciliation
exampleSteps.description: Check the external system before repeating or continuing an uncertain action.

theory.takeaway: Every long task must restart safely from saved and verified progress.

theory.heading: Use logs, metrics, and traces for different questions
paragraphs: A log records one event. A metric is a number measured over time, such as failure count. A trace connects the steps of one request from start to finish.

Carry one correlation identifier through context building, search, model calls, approvals, execution, and verification. OpenTelemetry is a standard for representing and sending this trace information.
Do not record secrets or full private documents by default. Observability data needs its own access, retention, and deletion rules.
theory.takeaway: Collect enough evidence to explain failures without creating a new privacy risk.

theory.heading: Plan for unavailable services and limited budgets
paragraphs: Resilience means continuing safely when a dependency is slow or unavailable. Use timeouts, limited retries, increasing delays, and circuit breakers. A circuit breaker temporarily stops calls to a failing service. Backpressure slows new work when the system is full.

Define a reduced but safe behavior for missing model or search services. Release model settings, instructions, tools, skills, policies, and schemas with versions, a small canary release, monitoring, and rollback.
Limit model usage, money, response time, simultaneous work, and operator time. Reduce cost only when evaluation gates still pass.
theory.takeaway: A system is reliable only when it fails safely and stays within its operating budget.

workedExample.title: Trace one request across the harness
workedExample.explanation: Child spans retain one correlation identifier from request through verification.
workedExample.code:
with tracer.start_as_current_span("printer.incident") as span:
    span.set_attribute("incident.id", incident.id)
    context = build_context(incident)
    proposal = model_call(context)
    decision = policy_check(proposal)
    result = tool_gateway(decision)
    verify(result)

practice.title: Make an incident resumable and observable
practice.scenario: A diagnosis waits for approval, the worker restarts, and the printer API briefly fails. Resume without duplicating the command.
tasks: Persist a checkpoint before waiting for approval.
Propagate a correlation identifier.
Use timeout, retry, reconciliation, and circuit-breaker behavior.
Record token, latency, and cost budgets.
codeGuide: incident_id is a stable identifier used to load saved progress; it must not depend on one running worker.
Reconcile the checkpoint with the external system before continuing an uncertain action.
Use one correlation identifier in the trace so the original request and resumed work remain connected.
Reuse the saved idempotency key when continuing the same command.
hints: Approval wait should be a durable state, not a blocked thread.
Query the printer before retrying an uncertain write.

quiz.question: Why persist before waiting for human approval?
options: To keep a worker thread busy
So the task can resume safely after delay or restart
So approval can change the command arguments
quiz.explanation: Approval can take minutes or hours; durable state makes the wait resumable and auditable.

completionEvidence: SLO, cost budget, canary, and rollback runbook

lessons.title: Uncertainty, Human Control, and Governance
lessons.summary: Make uncertain systems safer by reducing authority, showing people clear evidence, and enforcing operational rules.
outcomes: Separate observing, planning, acting, checking, selecting information, knowledge, and memory.
Use measured confidence and missing-information categories to route actions safely.
Choose where a person approves, monitors, or is not required for low-risk work.
Add emergency stop, separate responsibilities, saved records, and incident review.

theory.heading: Less evidence must never create more permission
paragraphs: Imagine a fraud-review assistant that cannot tell whether a card payment belongs to the customer. The safe response is to request more evidence or ask a person, not to gain permission to block every account.
Confidence is a measured signal compared with past results, not a feeling written by the model. A known unknown is a specific missing fact. A conflict means two trusted facts disagree.
Map each uncertainty state to fixed behavior: another approved read, a safe refusal, or human review. Never unlock broader tools or skip approval.

exampleSteps.name: Classify the evidence — knowledge state
exampleSteps.description: Mark the case as sufficient, missing information, conflicting information, or unsupported.

exampleSteps.name: Apply fixed thresholds — confidence gate
exampleSteps.description: A gate is code that routes low-confidence cases away from automatic action.

exampleSteps.name: Choose a safer route — escalation
exampleSteps.description: Request approved evidence or human review while keeping permissions unchanged or smaller.

theory.takeaway: As uncertainty increases, permission stays the same or becomes smaller.

theory.heading: Choose exactly where a person controls the process
paragraphs: Human-in-the-loop means a person must approve before an action. Human-on-the-loop means limited automation runs while a person monitors and can stop it. Human-out-of-the-loop means no person reviews each action and is suitable only for low-risk, tightly limited behavior.

An approval screen must show the proposed action, evidence, uncertainty, risk, expected result, and alternatives. Measure how often people are interrupted and whether their decisions are careful. Too many requests encourage automatic approval without review.
theory.takeaway: Human control works only when the person receives clear evidence and a meaningful choice.

theory.heading: Turn governance rules into executable behavior
paragraphs: Policy as code means writing permissions, data-retention rules, model-use rules, and release gates as testable software. Separation of duties means different people or systems propose, approve, and deploy sensitive changes.

Prepare an emergency stop and an incident plan before using real hardware. The plan covers severity, containment, recovery, communication, and review. Turn lessons from an incident into tests, evaluations, or policy changes.
theory.takeaway: Governance is real when rules are enforced by code and practiced during incidents.

workedExample.title: Gate action by evidence and confidence
workedExample.explanation: Uncertainty routes the action to observation or a human instead of increasing autonomy.

practice.title: Implement confidence-gated operations
practice.scenario: The printer advisor has incomplete evidence about a possible thermal issue. Route the case safely and preserve operator evidence.
tasks: Classify known, unknown, and conflicting evidence.
Map uncertainty to observation, abstention, or escalation.
Require approval for high-risk action regardless of confidence.
Add an emergency-stop policy path.
Implement deterministic confidence-gate and stagnation-detector functions, then test declining confidence, repeated evidence, and unresolved contradiction.
codeGuide: Evaluate the emergency-stop state first because it overrides normal routing.
Represent knowledge state and terminal routes with enums or typed result classes, not magic strings.
Check evidence conflict and confidence before evaluating an action for execution.
High confidence never removes approval required by risk policy.
hints: Evaluate emergency-stop state before normal routing.
High confidence does not remove risk-based approval.

quiz.question: What should low confidence do to an agent's permissions?
options: Increase them so the agent can investigate freely
Reduce or preserve them while routing to safe observation or escalation
quiz.explanation: Uncertainty may narrow behavior or trigger escalation, but it must never expand authority.

completionEvidence: Confidence-gate and stagnation tests proving that uncertainty never expands authority

lessons.title: Final Production Review and Reuse
lessons.summary: Test the complete system, collect release evidence, and prove reusable parts with one second business example.
outcomes: Connect fixed rules, AI recommendations, security, evaluations, and production operations.
Decide release readiness from testable evidence.
Create operating instructions, security analysis, decision records, and clear ownership.
Reuse proven components in a second domain without copying printer-specific rules.

theory.heading: Parts that work alone can still fail together
paragraphs: A car's engine, brakes, and steering can each pass a separate test while the complete car still has a dangerous connection problem. Software components can fail in the same way at the places where they connect.

A context version may not match a tool schema. Approval may expire during a retry. Saved memory may contain an older policy. The capstone tests complete success and failure journeys across code, models, tools, data, people, and infrastructure.

An architecture fitness function is an automated check for an architecture rule. Examples include dependency direction, permission boundaries, trace identifiers, evaluation gates, and recovery behavior.

exampleSteps.name: Test one complete journey — end-to-end test
exampleSteps.description: Start with a user request and continue through model, policy, approval, action, and verification.

exampleSteps.name: Break one dependency — failure journey
exampleSteps.description: Repeat the journey while a required service is unavailable and verify the safe result.

exampleSteps.name: Check architecture rules — fitness functions
exampleSteps.description: Automatically prove that privileged actions still cross the required permission boundary.

theory.takeaway: Production readiness belongs to the complete system, not to isolated components.

theory.heading: A production service needs an owner and recovery evidence
paragraphs: A readiness review is a structured decision about whether a service may be released. It asks who owns it, how reliability is measured, how incidents are detected, which actions need approval, how data is kept, and how a release is reversed.

Support every answer with tests, evaluation reports, traces, threat models, cost estimates, and practiced recovery steps. Also document how typed contracts, policies, memory, and evaluations can move if the model provider or SDK changes.
theory.takeaway: Without an owner, evidence, and tested recovery, the system remains a prototype.

theory.heading: A second domain proves what is really reusable
paragraphs: A reusable name does not prove reusable design. Apply one thin end-to-end path to a second domain, such as online-order operations.

Reuse policy, approval, audit, evaluation, tracing, and harness contracts only where they truly fit. Replace the printer states, commands, risks, and source knowledge with the second domain's concepts.
Record what moved unchanged, what needed adaptation, and what should have remained specific. This evidence reveals the real module boundaries.
theory.takeaway: Prove reuse with a working second example, not with generic class names.

workedExample.title: Define a readiness gate
workedExample.explanation: The release is blocked unless every critical evidence category passes.
workedExample.code:
readiness = ReadinessReview(
    deterministic_tests=PASS,
    agent_evals=PASS,
    safety_gate=PASS,
    threat_model=APPROVED,
    recovery_drill=PASS,
    rollback_drill=PASS,
    service_owner="operations-platform-team",
)
readiness.require_all_critical()

practice.title: Assemble the production evidence pack
practice.scenario: Prepare the printer platform for a simulated production review and define a second-domain vertical slice.
tasks: Collect architecture, test, eval, trace, security, cost, and recovery evidence.
Define SLOs, ownership, alerts, runbooks, and rollback.
Run an incident and recovery drill.
Map reusable and domain-specific components for a second operational domain.

commandGuide.terminal: On Windows 10 or 11, open PowerShell. On Ubuntu, open Bash in the Terminal application. First activate the project's virtual environment.
commandGuide.why: Run the complete automated test suite first. Run the production-readiness review only when every test passes.
commandGuide.program: pytest runs Python tests. python starts the Python interpreter. The readiness-review module performs the project's final evidence checks.
parts: pytest -q runs all tests and shows short output.
In PowerShell, the semicolon ends the first command. $LASTEXITCODE contains its result. -eq 0 checks that pytest succeeded before PowerShell runs the code inside braces.
In Ubuntu Bash, && runs the second command only when the first command succeeds.
python -m asks Python to run an installed module by name.
tools.run_readiness_review is the module that performs the readiness review.
commandGuide.effect: The commands read the repository, run tests, and produce readiness-review output. They may create test cache and review-result files inside the repository. They do not permanently change Windows or Ubuntu.
commandGuide.successEvidence: All tests report passed, and the readiness review reports that every critical gate passed. Any failed critical gate means the release is not ready.
commandGuide.failureRecovery: If pytest is not found, confirm that the virtual environment is active. If tests fail, fix the first failure before running the readiness review. If the module is not found, confirm that you are in the repository root and that the project dependencies are installed.

codeGuide: evidence_pack is a typed collection of test, evaluation, security, recovery, ownership, and release evidence.
Represent critical evidence categories with an enum instead of a list of magic strings.
Return a typed Ready or Blocked result containing exact failed categories.
A warning may carry an owner and deadline, but any failed critical gate must block release.
hints: A warning can be accepted only with an owner and deadline; critical failures block release.
The second domain needs one thin end-to-end slice, not a second full product.

quiz.question: What best proves that an architecture is transferable?
options: Adding more interfaces before a second use case
Implementing a second-domain vertical slice and comparing what actually transfers
quiz.explanation: A real second domain reveals the correct reusable seams and the abstractions that were premature.

completionEvidence: Passed readiness review and operational runbook
Incident, recovery, emergency-stop, and rollback drills
Second-domain vertical slice and transfer ADR
